data.title: Carl Zeiss Meditec: Bad Optics But With 'Buy' Potential
data.description:
Earlier this year, I started the expansion of my coverage of healthcare equipment names. Most readers may know me from coverage of aerospace and defense as well as semiconductors, but there is more overlap with healthcare equipment than many investors think. In this report, I discuss the investment case for Carl Zeiss Meditec AG (CZMWF), which interestingly shows that geopolitics are impacting the business.

High-Tech And Low Error Tolerance Bind Healthcare With Aerospace

While it may not be immediately clear that there is an overlap between aerospace and defense and healthcare equipment names, there actually is a lot of overlap.

In fact, several aerospace and defense companies maintain direct exposure to medical technology markets or leverage similar engineering capabilities across both sectors. From a research perspective, analyzing companies in the healthcare equipment market therefore provides valuable insight into adjacent high-technology markets. This perspective is also reinforced by my academic background. As part of my aerospace and defense education, I pursued a minor track in MedTech-based Entrepreneurship, which focused on the development, commercialization, and financing of medical technologies.

Looking Beyond The Generic Investment Thesis

The generic investment thesis for many manufacturers of healthcare equipment is that life expectancy is increasing and treatment methods and tools are getting more advanced as technology and medical innovation also advance. However, with the complexity, there is also a cost attached, and what we are seeing now with Carl Zeiss Meditec is that sales to China are under pressure and capital expenditures are being pushed out as a function of macro and healthcare budgets. While geopolitics is not necessarily a root cause, it does serve as an amplifier. Tariffs and forex effects can creep up in the supply chain.

China accounts for roughly a quarter of the sales, and that is a state-regulated purchase process, as buying in bulk provides a stronger position to negotiate discounts. When those orders stall due to geopolitical flexing or simply because hospitals are looking to commit to capital expenditures when policies are clearer, it can affect healthcare equipment manufacturers. So, the investment case is not as simple as “people get older and equipment gets more advanced, so the sky is the limit.” There are other factors, such as the timing of orders and macro pressures, that also play a role.

Carl Zeiss Meditec has two segments:

  1. Ophthalmology
    1. Providing imaging systems such as scanners.
    2. Providing surgical systems for laser eye surgery and cataract surgery.
    3. Consumables such as intraocular lenses.
  2. Microsurgery
    1. Providing microscopes used for neurosurgery, ENT (ear, nose, and throat), and spine surgery.

The business has recurring revenue drivers in the form of consumables, service contracts, and software digital platform revenues that layer on top of the revenues from the production of tools and medical devices.

Weak Start Of The Year Triggers Outlook Suspension

In the first quarter, sales declined by 4.8% to €467 million, driven by a 5.1% decrease in Ophthalmology sales to €356.9 million and Microsurgery sales declining 3.7% to €110.1 million. We note that the FX-adjusted revenues for these segments showed declines of 2.4% and 0.9%. So, a substantial portion of the decline was driven by changes in the currency exchange rates. Besides that, the intraocular lenses business was impacted by the withdrawal of bifocal lenses from the volume-based procurement tender in China and the timing of the Chinese New Year delaying treatment sales. The Microsurgery segment saw a reduction in sales driven by the comparable period last year having strong equipment deliveries, which remained absent this year.

Gross margins reduced by 2.8 points to 48.6%, while EBITA tumbled 77% to €8.1 billion, indicating a margin drop of 5.5 points to 1.7%. On an adjusted basis, things did not look much better, with a 69% decline in adjusted EBITA to €10.3 million and margins dropping 4.5 points to 2.2%. So, we are seeing that the company had a weak start to the year, and this was not just driven by China but also by weakness in the overall Asia-Pacific region and North America. The drop was bad enough for Carl Zeiss Meditech to remove its 2025/2026 outlook targeting €2.3 billion in sales with EBITA margins of 12.5%.

Carl Zeiss Meditec Stock Initiated With Buy Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: Price targets are based on the ordinary shares traded in Frankfurt under the ticker AFX converted to USD. The stock also is available under the OTC tickers CZMWY and CZMWF.

We note that Carl Zeiss Meditec has lost over 80% over the past five years, and that is driven by the fact that the company used to trade as a medtech premium optics company, but growth fell behind expectations and resulted in a multi-year downtrend. That is also the risk for Carl Zeiss Meditec, but we note that we are now valuing the stock at an EV/EBITDA multiple below that of the peer group and at a third of the company’s longer-term EV/EBITDA. This closely aligns with where the stock price is trading now with 2026 earnings in mind. That would allow for roughly 20% upside with a $39.26 price target when looking at earnings a year ahead.

The CAGR on sales is not impressive at just 3.5%, but EBITDA is expected to show 7% annualized growth and 12% annualized free cash flow growth, with the note that this year will likely largely be a flat year for the company.

If we look at the margin profile, we can see that margins show a bit of a bathtub shape with partial recovery towards 2028. For this year, the expectation is that free cash flow will be more or less stable with growth in the years ahead. Capital intensity is around 40% of operating cash flow in the coming years, with a reduction to 33% by 2028. The company has a €400 million loan from the Zeiss Group, and that is the only meaningful debt it has. So, if Carl Zeiss Meditec executes, we should see significant improvement in the company’s net-debt leverage, which was already not in a horrible position to start with, even more so given that the loan is obtained from the Zeiss Group and likely includes flexible repayment terms.

Conclusion: Carl Zeiss Meditec Is Not A Great Company But Still A "Buy"

Carl Zeiss Meditec is unlikely to be my favorite play for healthcare equipment. The company has high exposure to China, but beyond there is overall hesitance in the Asia-Pacific and North American regions, and Carl Zeiss Meditec simply has not lived up to its premium valuation levels. However, even when priced at a discount to peers, I still see upside, and if the market recovers, I believe the stock could appreciate. As a result, I am marking the stock a "Buy."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VBR: Leading Passive Small Value ETF
data.description:
Fast Facts About The Vanguard Small-Cap Value Index Fund ETF

The Vanguard Small-Cap Value Index Fund ETF (VBR) was launched on January 26, 2004, and tracks the CRSP US Small Cap Value Index. VBR has a portfolio of 838 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 2.00%, and an expense ratio of 0.05% (recently down from 0.07%). It is a very large and liquid ETF, with $32.6 billion of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $78 million. Dividends are paid quarterly. It is also available as a mutual fund (VSIAX).

The sponsor, Vanguard Group, was founded in 1975 and has about $12 trillion in total AUM. Vanguard has a unique ownership structure: it is owned by its U.S.-based funds, which are owned by their shareholders.

Strategy

As described by CRSP, the underlying index…

Represents the Value Style for companies covering 85% to 98% of cumulative capitalization of CRSP US Total Market.

Stocks are classified as “Value” based on metrics such as price-to-book, price-to-earnings (historical and forward), price-to-sales ratios, and dividend yield. The index is weighted based on free-float market capitalization and reconstituted on a quarterly basis. The portfolio turnover rate was 16% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark the parent index CRSP US Small Cap Index, represented by Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (VB).

Portfolio

The fund has significant exposure in industrials (21.6% of asset value), financials (18.2%), and consumer discretionary (14%). Other sectors are below 10%. Compared to the parent index, VBR mostly overweights financials and downplays technology, healthcare, and telecommunications.

The fund has very low company-specific risk. The top 10 holdings are listed below and only represent 6.1% of asset value, with the heaviest position weighing 0.75%.

Most value funds have underperformed their benchmarks for over a decade. It is not a deal breaker: the value investing style is likely to shine again at some point in the market cycle.

Although VBR has a low yield and is not designed to attract dividend investors, it boasts strong dividend growth. The annual sum of distributions has increased by 111% between 2015 and 2025, greatly outpacing cumulative inflation (about 38% in the same time, based on CPI).

Competitors

The next table compares characteristics of VBR and four of the largest U.S. small-cap value ETFs:

  • Avantis U.S. Small Cap Value ETF (AVUV).
  • Dimensional U.S. Targeted Value ETF (DFAT).

VBR is the largest of these funds and has the lowest expense ratio. It comes in third position for dollar trading volumes, a clue that it attracts more long-term investors than traders. Based on total return and Sharpe ratio since June 2021, VBR is the best performer among the passively managed ETFs on this list. It also shows the lowest risk metrics (volatility and drawdown). However, it significantly underperforms the active funds AVUV and DFAT. These are not strictly single-factor value ETFs, as they also use profitability metrics in their selection process.

Takeaway

The Vanguard Small-Cap Value Index Fund ETF has a portfolio of over 800 small U.S. companies selected based on value metrics, with sector diversification and very low company risk. VBR is well-suited for investors seeking low-cost exposure to the value factor in small caps for long-term or tactical allocation. Nonetheless, some active small-cap ETFs, such as AVUV and DFAT, offer more attractive returns by blending value and quality.

  • Pros: Low cost, low volatility, diversification, outperforms passive ETF peers.
  • Cons: Lags some active ETFs.

This article answers these three main questions about VBR:

  1. How diversified is the VBR portfolio?
  2. How does VBR compare to benchmarks and peers?
  3. What type of strategy is VBR best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: GSST: Goldman Fund Delivering On Its Promises
data.description:
Thesis

It has been a year since we last covered the Goldman Sachs Ultra Short Bond ETF (GSST), and we are going to do an annual review on the name and its rating. The ETF falls in the short-term bond fund bucket and represents a way to park cash while clipping an enhanced yield when compared to treasuries. In today's article we are going to focus on whether GSST has been able to achieve that and if investors are well served to put their capital to work via GSST.

What Do Short-Term Bond Funds Do?

Short-term bond funds exist in order to enhance yields while keeping risks contained. Investors have realized they can get anywhere from 20 bps to 80 bps over T-Bills by parking cash in short-term bond funds, figures which are becoming more important as rates move lower. One of the key features is the risks taken and the volatility posted. Some funds take more credit risk, while others take more duration risk to generate additional yield. The best-run funds take a little of both but, more importantly, are really, really good at managing the downside (i.e., the risk profile) for such a fund.

As Rates Move Lower, Short-Term Bond Funds Are Becoming More Attractive

While the market can debate ad nauseam what the Fed will do in 2026, it should be clear by now from the Fed dot plot that we are in a monetary easing environment where rates are headed lower:

How low they will go is a matter of debate, but when looking at the above chart, we can see a potential neutral rate at 3%. In a net low-rate environment, 50 bps becomes much more important than the same 50 bps when Fed Funds are at 6%, for example. Hence the appeal of short-term bond funds.

Also, please remember the political aspect embedded in today's environment, with President Trump being very clear that he is expecting much lower Fed Funds:

Earlier this week, Trump repeated his assertion that interest rates should be much lower than the current range of 3.5% to 3.75%. Asked during an interview on Fox Business Network if he thought rates would go lower later this year, Trump replied: "When Kevin gets in, I do."

If the Iran war is solved quickly, the market will shift back expectations to what we saw in the beginning of the year, which is 1 or 2 Fed cuts in 2026. The political will is clearly tilted towards more cuts, but it is unclear how the new Fed chair will be able to implement that view.

As Fed Funds move lower, T-Bills will continue to deliver smaller and smaller returns, thus making short-dated bond funds with excess spreads more attractive to investors.

GSST Has Been Able to Deliver a Low-Volatility Profile

For us, one of the most important factors when looking at a fund like GSST is its ability to deliver on a low-risk requirement. We do not like funds which have a high standard deviation because they no longer serve the purpose of value preservation but rather act as risk-taking instruments.

When looking at GSST, we can see the ETF posting very low volatility figures:

As per the Seeking Alpha 'Risk' tab, the ETF's standard deviation is a mere 0.69%, while the annualized volatility is 0.6%. As a comparison point, the iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV) has a standard deviation of 0.44% and an annualized volatility of 0.2%. GSST, therefore, has delivered on a low volatility mandate.

GSST Has Delivered on an Excess Spread as Well

When looking at total returns, we also want to make sure that GSST has been able to deliver on its excess spread mandate:

When comparing GSST to purely treasuries or T-Bill funds, we can see a massive outperformance. In the past year, GSST has posted a total return of 4.77%, all while SGOV posted 4.03% and BIL posted 3.94%. The Goldman fund, therefore, has provided roughly 75 to 80 bps of excess return over the competition with minimal volatility impact. This is a massive result, and it speaks volumes to the ability of the fund managers to take smart risks and adjust exposures.

Furthermore, when looking at the above chart, we see exactly how a short-dated bond fund should perform, consistently outperforming its peers with a very low volatility profile. If, for example, GSST would have had a poor build, its drawdown during the most recent market sell-off would have been much greater.

Current Fund Composition

Currently the ETF's composition is as follows:

The name currently clips its excess spread via a large investment-grade corporate bucket, which sits at 55%. The next largest segment is ABS at 14.2%, followed by commercial paper at 9.3%.

The overall portfolio duration is a mere 0.5 years, highlighting that the name does not take significant credit or rates risk. Additional fund analytics are as follows:

  • AUM: $1.47 billion
  • Expense ratio: 0.16%
  • SEC yield: 4.1%

From a ratings perspective, the fund is exclusively investment grade, as expected:

GSST contains a large single-A sleeve and only 21% BBB names (the riskiest investment-grade bonds).

Risk Factors

The largest risk factor for this name is a severe and prolonged recession, similar to 2008. While the fund duration is very, very low, credit events do happen during massive recessions. For example, in 2008, the investment-grade-rated Lehman Brothers went into bankruptcy. Even in that scenario, the fund should be able to manage any potential defaults via its granular approach to its build, but it nonetheless does represent a risk factor. From an individual corporate issuer perspective, no issuer represents more than 1% of the fund, meaning that an issuer default would only cause the fund's excess spread to move lower by 1%. This is a very outside scenario, but the main risk factor to consider.

Conclusion

GSST is a short-dated bond fund from Goldman. The name has done an outstanding job since its inception in providing for excess spreads over treasuries while maintaining a low volatility profile. GSST and T Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF (TBUX) are our go-to names for cash parking and clipping a higher yield while waiting for attractive investment opportunities.

data.title: VONV ETF: Missing The Mark Within The Value Sector
data.description:
VONV At A Glance

The Vanguard Russell 1000 Value Index Fund ETF (VONV) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund that invests in U.S. large-cap stocks to construct an equity portfolio that looks at multiple value factors. When analyzing this ETF, it's clear that the fund has underperformed and holds too many stocks that look good from a fundamental theme but have negative qualitative factors. It's why I like to approach owning stocks that I understand and funds that track sectors that have distinct themes about them, and not just pick funds that have value-oriented tilts.

The point of this ETF is to have a passively managed option to track the returns of value stocks within the U.S. market, with the fund manager rebalancing and reviewing the fund often for investment opportunities. This fund is specifically a replication fund, which means it tracks the Russell rebalancing moves and then makes the exact same buys and sells to give investors an easy, low-fee way to access this group of stocks.

VONV's benchmark target is the Russell 1000 Value Index. The Russell 1000 Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the US equity universe. This benchmark includes Russell 1000 companies with select criteria, including: relatively lower price-to-book ratios, lower forecast medium-term (2-year) growth, and lower sales per share historical growth (5 years).

After further review, my analysis shows that VONV is not worth an investment versus other passively managed peers. I've written in the past about value ETFs, highlighting that you have to be careful in how the portfolio is constructed because so many value traps (e.g., PayPal) hide in plain sight with attractive numbers but terrible qualitative business fundamentals. This fund has underperformed other Russell benchmarks over the last two months as the war has played out, and I recommend investors buy funds like iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB)or the iShares Russell Top 200 ETF (IWL) for passive exposure to similar benchmarks that have both value and growth - and cherry-pick the best value portion for the portfolio. While the fund has some specific criteria that in theory makes sense, I don't love the recent performance and rate it a hold.

2026 Q2 Outlook

VONV was launched on September 20, 2010, by Vanguard with a mandate to evaluate the top securities in an investment universe comprised of U.S. value stocks and to build an equity portfolio that looks to achieve exposure to large-cap value. And the trade has had its moments - the growth vs. value story is sensitive to macro interest rate cycle shifts. Notably, during the 2022 tightening cycle, rising real yields compressed long-duration growth valuations and supported value sectors (financials, energy, industrials).

Additionally, market breadth has remained narrow for years now, with a few tech stocks driving the majority of overall market returns. In these situations, cap-weighted indexes usually outperform equal-weight and value-tilted approaches due to heavier exposure to market leaders, and it aligns with the K-shaped recovery in consumers as well. From a fund standpoint, the idea behind the approach is to promote diversification and to mitigate exposure to certain over-weighted large-cap stocks. In theory, the mandate makes sense, but I'm a bit dubious of these passively managed funds that try to find specific "growth" or "value" within these larger, distinct buckets (i.e., small-cap, mid-cap, etc.). If you look at the returns on some of these funds, VONV included, there isn't always a link to see the risk vs. reward returns that are mentioned in the prospectus strategy as the target.

While, the fund reasonably provides its mandate, and the portfolio has had a 17% turnover, which is in line with the change in the benchmark rebalancing. The problem is, though, that the benchmark screens out quality. Sure, the price-to-book is lower than the normal benchmark, and the P/E is only 20.7 vs. 25.4, but the EPS growth is over 15% lower, and the fund returns have been materially worse with limited dividends to support investors. Simply put, your average company in this index is likely more poorly run than in the mainstream indexes, and your risk is almost the same (more on the beta and other quantitative ratios in a moment).

One upside regarding the fund is that it has been trading for years, with a NAV of $18 billion and a 50-day daily volume of over 1.6 million shares traded, worth over $160 million in daily volume. However, the fund has simply not outperformed over any meaningful time, as noted in my last article, and I'm skeptical that the fund has been a good steward of investors' capital as volatility has emerged in the markets due to the war. Below is a chart of the past two months, showing returns well below other passive Russell index funds with similar low fee structures under 0.1%.

Fund Portfolio and Risk Profile

When comparing VONV's portfolio to peers, there are some differences in the portfolio breakdown that skew value and quality. As of Mar. 31, the portfolio holds stocks with an average P/E of ~20x and have balanced exposures, which makes sense given the war. But I think amidst everything, it simply hasn't mattered - even with the recent rally after expectations of the ceasefire would hold, the bottom line is this fund has pretty much been stuck in the mud, often times with larger drawdowns and underperformance on the bouncebacks.

Meanwhile, the fund has been weighted more significantly in financials and industrials versus other peer indexes, while reducing its tech holdings. Ultimately, the theme of value isn't a bad one, but when you go all in on "value," you find out there's usually a reason these stocks have been left for dead by investors. And the top holdings in the portfolio likely should have had higher weightings.

Risks

From a risk perspective, a beta of 0.87 notes that the fund has a bit less volatility than the benchmark. This is not surprising and validated in the fund facts, given that value stocks should be a bit less volatile over time. Additionally, when evaluating the fund's risk-adjusted returns, I analyzed the Sharpe ratio to see whether the fund has generated excess returns over the risk-free rate when adjusted for total volatility. Unfortunately, the Sharpe ratio for the fund is 0.74, below the score of 1+ to prove strong risk-adjusted returns (and slightly below the category average), but not awful. However, given recent stock market gains, a number under 1 is concerning.

While I agree that having some value is a good way to have equity exposure, I'd rather buy IWL or IWB, as the funds have a cleaner basket of stocks, only marginally higher volatility, and significantly higher risk-adjusted returns. There is a risk that funds can become a self-fulfilling prophecy - and simply not be an interest for investors that leads to lack of support.

Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that there are better options for low-fee value ETFs on the market. There's simply not enough good news to justify buying this fund against other low-fee peers, and while I understand the allure of value-focused ETFs, often times, there are too many low-quality businesses to make it a focal point of a portfolio. While the idea makes sense, given the war, there are other ways to keep a lower-risk portfolio as the ceasefire negotiations play out. It's worth keeping on the watch list, but it's not currently an investment for me when analyzing a broad-based equity market portfolio.

data.title: Ivanhoe Electric: High-Risk, High-Reward Bet On The Copper Boom
data.description:
Investment thesis

Ivanhoe Electric Inc. (IE) has doubled from the prior time I covered this stock for Seeking Alpha, but its trajectory has been anything but linear, even when compared to the volatile Sprott Junior Copper Miners ETF (COPJ):

Ivanhoe Electric is a pre-revenue copper exploration company. Its flagship asset is the Santa Cruz project in Arizona. The preliminary feasibility study from 2025 estimated Santa Cruz can produce 1.4 million tons of copper over a 23-year mine life.

Ivanhoe Electric is far more than a junior exploration company, though. It owns two proprietary exploration technologies, Typhoon and CGI, that enhance the exploration process. For now, the company primarily monetizes its technologies through JVs with large mining players. In addition, through its 90% owned VRB Energy subsidiary, Ivanhoe develops energy storage systems based on vanadium redox batteries.

The investment thesis for Ivanhoe Electric is simple:

  • Copper is in a structural bull market, and miners won't be able to meet future demand without incremental exploration;
  • The net present value (or NPV) of the Santa Cruz asset alone exceeds Ivanhoe's enterprise value (or EV);
  • Typhoon, CGI, the JVs and the vanadium business are next to impossible to quantify, but investors essentially get them for free.

As the company now projects first copper cathode production from Santa Cruz in 2029, Ivanhoe Electric should be considered a highly speculative investment. However, the insider and strategic entity ownership, as well as the JVs with established mining powerhouses should be seen as risk-mitigating factors.

The copper supply crunch is just starting

Copper (HG1:COM) has been steadily grinding up since the pandemic:

Unlike the precious metals complex, which is undergoing a correction from the recent tops, copper seems unphased by the events around the Strait of Hormuz.

There are two sides to the copper thesis: (1) greater demand, due to the electrification of everything, and, perhaps more importantly, (2) less supply, due to chronic underinvestment into exploration and new mine development. Recently, a number of supply-negative events have transpired too:

  • In September, Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) suspended production at its Grasberg mine in Indonesia after a major incident;
  • The Cobre mine in Panama remains shut despite some progress reported towards eventual reopening;
  • Codelco of Chile, the largest global producer, cut guidance in part due to an incident at its El Teniente mine.

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz hasn't been helpful either. Codelco reported earlier this month that the war had pushed up its cash costs by 10 cents per pound. Apart from fuel, sulfuric acid appears to be a big problem as Robert Friedland, IE's founder and chairman, himself recently warned.

Meanwhile, demand projections remain strong. BHP (BHP), the mining powerhouse, sees 70% growth over the next 25 years:

While we are likely past the "peak ESG" point, electrification may get a renewed push from the energy security angle given the vulnerability of oil supplies demonstrated by Hormuz. Data center demand will probably also remain strong though a correction to the AI trade is long overdue.

As mines are aging and copper grades declining, the only answer is exploration:

And this is where Ivanhoe Electric comes in with its flagship Arizona project and exploration technology portfolio.

Santa Cruz due for 2029 production start

As reported by Seeking Alpha, Ivanhoe completed its preliminary feasibility study for Santa Cruz last summer:

Highlights of the preliminary feasibility study include probable mineral reserves of 136M metric tons at a grade of 1.08% copper totaling 1.5M tons of contained copper supporting a 23-year mine life.

Source: Seeking Alpha

Financing doesn't seem to be an issue. The company has also received a letter of interest from the Export-Import Bank of the United States for potential $825 million debt financing to help with the development. As reported, the project aligns with the EXIM Bank's "Make More in America" initiative, intended to grow the U.S. supply of critical minerals.

In October, Ivanhoe announced a $150 million public offering, which was upsized from the initial $125 million due to investor interest. The offering was priced at $15 per share, which was close to where the stock traded in the market at the time of the announcement.

Technology and JVs

The company describes its Typhoon technology as its "proprietary electrical geophysical surveying transmitter" which can detect minerals containing copper or other metals at greater depths than conventional systems or in areas where deposits are hidden by cover (rock type or other impediment that blocks signal). Allegedly, Typhoon allows Ivanhoe to see deposits at depths of up to one and a half kilometers or more, which is 3-5x more than what conventional systems can do.

Typhoon is complemented by CGI, which is essentially software that processes the geophysical data to build 3-D subsurface images. CGI is also made available via a software-as-a-service model to customers in the minerals and energy exploration areas.

While the technology can be monetized directly, Ivanhoe's strategy appears to be using Typhoon/CGI to get an equity stake in joint exploration projects with established miners:

  • The exploration alliance with BHP focuses on Arizona, New Mexico, and Utah. BHP has provided the $15 million initial funding while Ivanhoe is making its technology available for a 50% stake;
  • The JV with the Saudi Arabian Mining Company (Maaden) involved Maaden providing the land and regulatory licenses; Maaden also acquired equity in Ivanhoe Electric, with Ivanhoe contributing the proceeds back to the JV for 50/50 stake;
  • The cooperation with Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile (SQM) involved SQM providing $9 million in upfront funding while Ivanhoe is making available Typhoon for a 50% stake.

None of the JVs will generate cash flow for Ivanhoe any time soon - and perhaps never if the JV exploration efforts don't transition into development. However, the JV strategy provides two long-term benefits: (1) it aligns the industry's powerhouses to Ivanhoe; (2) it allows major industry participants to get familiar with the technology, which should translate into more long-term demand for its use.

Lastly, the vanadium batteries developed by VRB Energy are also an interesting project. Vanadium batteries allegedly last longer than lithium-ion batteries and can bring down the levelized cost of electricity for renewables production systems that rely on storage:

The vanadium battery business aligns with the energy security and U.S. critical minerals access themes, although VRB Energy appears to have significant exposure to China, something that may be a risk if US-China tensions in the critical minerals area continue.

Valuation

Typhoon, CGI or the JV stakes aren't possible to value reliably. These are all mostly pre-revenue ventures, and Ivanhoe's strategy seems to prefer equity participation to simply selling the technology access to another explorer. This is why I see these ventures as "upside" that can range from zero to potentially a very high number.

The relatively tangible piece of the business is the Santa Cruz asset. At $4.25 per pound of copper, Ivanhoe's feasibility estimates $1.4 billion NPV:

However, copper is closer to $6 now, so based on the sensitivities provided, we are likely looking at NPV of around $3 billion instead.

The enterprise value reported by Seeking Alpha is $2.3 billion, so this implies 30% upside (the company doesn't have material debt). This is just Santa Cruz, though, without assigning any value to the technology of JVs. Wall Street analyst consensus is for 40% upside:

This is not unreasonable, especially considering we recently traded there. Ivanhoe Electric is very volatile, though, so I would probably prefer to accumulate in the $11-$12 range to limit downside risk:

If the stock keeps pushing higher, I will look for a new entry point.

Risks

The main risk is that Ivanhoe Electric is an exploration-stage company. It may never generate meaningful cash flow. Copper prices are also a risk. Despite the structural bull trend, a recession could cyclically reduce the demand for copper.

Insider ownership by Robert Friedland and Saudi's Maaden is rather a risk-limiting factor. However, Saudi Arabia's involvement may become a negative if the tensions around Iran continue. Ultimately, Saudi Arabia's ability to finance copper exploration projects depends on its overall budgetary position.

Sulfuric acid prices aren't a direct risk to Ivanhoe Electric as there is no production yet. However, it may impact the company's JV partners.

Takeaway

Ivanhoe Electric is a high-risk, high-reward bet on the continued growth in copper demand while global mining output keeps disappointing. The upside isn't possible to quantify, but even Santa Cruz alone, which is the relatively tangible part of the business, suggests Ivanhoe Electric is undervalued for a $6 copper world.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: POET Technologies: Huge Risk, Massive Upside
data.description:
Thesis Summary

POET Technologies Inc. (POET) is an early and unproven AI stock. And yet, investors are already jumping into this growth story.

POET promises to deliver a new technological solution to one of the clearest AI bottlenecks, optical interconnects.

And 2026 could be the year we finally see everything come together.

If POET executes, the upside is significant given its positioning in what could become a critical AI bottleneck.

Of course, if it doesn’t, the downside could also be significant.

It's a high-risk, high-reward play, but it’s still early days, and the market has an appetite for these stocks right now.

What POET Actually Does

POET addresses one of the key bottlenecks in AI through an innovative technology.

POET’s the Optical Interposer, a platform designed to simplify the way optical engines are assembled.

The core idea is to replace that with a more semiconductor-like approach.

Instead of manually aligning components, the Optical Interposer uses lithographically defined structures to position everything in place.

In theory, this approach should greatly increase scalability while also lowering overall costs.

It changes the manufacturing model entirely.

2026 Is The Inflection Point

For the first time, POET appears to have the necessary ingredients to attempt a real transition into production.

The company has raised capital, built out its product portfolio, and established manufacturing partnerships, and has even secured initial orders, including a $50M 800G Infinity order.

The momentum in this sector has also been validated by Marvell's (MRVL) acquisition of Celestial AI, a company which shares a lot of similarities with POET.

2026 is going to be a key inflection point, as orders ramp up, but with it comes increased execution risk.

Is This The Micron Moment?

Ultimately, there are two key ingredients here that make POET compelling.

  • A large total addressable market, or TAM;

  • A revolutionary technology.

As we can see above, the Optical Interconnect market is expected to grow at a 12% CAGR until 2031, reaching a total market size of over $33B.

POET’s current annual revenues are just over $1M, with perhaps $10M expected by the end of the year.

This means the opportunity here is huge. Even if POET can scale to just a few billion per year.

Valuation: Pricing The Inflection

POET is expected to essentially go from no revenue to $10M in 2026 to $80M in 2027.

However, it’s important to note that this is based on one analyst. The truth is that there is not a lot of coverage of POET, and this is an early-stage company.

But the potential is there, and it’s not impossible to imagine a scenario like the one below.

Year

Revenue ($M)

Growth

EV @ 5x

EV @ 8x

EV @ 10x

2026

10

—

50M

80M

100M

2027

50

5x

400M

500M

2028

3x

750M

1.2B

1.5B

2029

400

2.7x

2.0B

3.2B

4.0B

1.75x

3.5B
